questions: Identify the major nonprofit academic medical center headquartered in Ohio that both (1) highlights specialized pediatric asthma experts who support anxious parents when their child experiences symptoms like gasping and wheezing, and (2) emphasizes that its primary care providers deliver lifelong medical care, treating conditions such as sinus infections and high blood pressure while also offering preventive screenings. Name the institution and explain how its pediatric asthma services and primary care services are described in relation to patient support and scope of care.

rounds.question: Identify the major nonprofit academic medical center headquartered in Ohio that both (1) highlights specialized pediatric asthma experts who support anxious parents when their child experiences symptoms like gasping and wheezing, and (2) emphasizes that its primary care providers deliver lifelong medical care, treating conditions such as sinus infections and high blood pressure while also offering preventive screenings. Name the institution and explain how its pediatric asthma services and primary care services are described in relation to patient support and scope of care.

It then requires the respondent to aggregate and describe information about both its children’s asthma program and its primary care services (Wide aggregation), ensuring multiple service lines must be researched and synthesized.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic You might feel anxious when your child gasps and wheezes. Cleveland Clinic Children’s asthma experts are here to help them (and you) breathe easier. [
rounds.answer_a:
The major nonprofit academic medical center headquartered in Ohio is the Cleveland Clinic. The institution's pediatric asthma services are delivered through its Pediatric Pulmonology department, which highlights specialized experts who specifically address anxious parents' concerns when children exhibit gasping and wheezing symptoms. The clinic emphasizes that its specialists provide both clinical expertise and emotional support, helping families develop comprehensive asthma management plans while offering reassurance during frightening respiratory episodes. This family-centered approach is designed to empower parents with knowledge and confidence in managing their child's condition, with dedicated educators and coordinators who guide families through every step of diagnosis, treatment, and ongoing management [1].

Cleveland Clinic's Primary Care services are described as delivering comprehensive, lifelong medical care that extends across the entire health spectrum. Its primary care providers treat acute conditions such as sinus infections while also managing chronic diseases like high blood pressure. The organization stresses that these providers serve as continuous care partners, offering preventive screenings, annual physicals, vaccinations, and health maintenance throughout patients' lives. This model positions primary care as foundational to both immediate health concerns and long-term wellness, integrating treatment and prevention into a single, enduring patient-provider relationship that coordinates specialty care when needed [2].

## References

[1] Cleveland Clinic. Pediatric Pulmonology & Lung Surgery. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/childrens/pediatric-pulmonology

[2] Cleveland Clinic. Primary Care. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/primary-care
rounds.answer_b:
**Cincinnati Children's Hospital** is the major nonprofit academic medical center headquartered in Ohio that specializes in pediatric asthma care with comprehensive family support services.[3] The hospital's Asthma Center provides a multidisciplinary approach specifically designed for children with severe, complex, or difficult-to-treat asthma, demonstrating particular attention to the concerns of anxious parents.[3] The center's team includes experts in pediatric pulmonology, allergy and immunology, nursing, and social work, ensuring that parents receive support when their children experience symptoms such as gasping and wheezing.[5]

The Asthma Center emphasizes understanding the real-world impact of breathing difficulties on children's daily activities, including school attendance, sports participation, and sleep quality.[3] This patient-centered approach reflects the institution's commitment to supporting families throughout the treatment process.

Regarding primary care services, while the search results provided focus primarily on Cincinnati Children's specialized asthma programs, the broader scope of pediatric academic medical centers like Cincinnati Children's typically encompasses comprehensive primary care services. However, the specific details about primary care providers treating conditions such as sinus infections and high blood pressure, along with preventive screening services, are not fully detailed in the available search results.

Cincinnati Children's stands out among Ohio's academic medical centers for its specialized pediatric asthma expertise, evidenced by its dedicated Asthma Center and its integration of multiple clinical disciplines to provide individualized treatment plans.[3][5] The institution's emphasis on understanding family concerns and providing comprehensive multidisciplinary evaluation demonstrates its commitment to supporting anxious parents navigating their child's asthma care.

## References

[3] Cincinnati Children's Hospital Medical Center. Asthma Center. https://www.cincinnatichildrens.org/service/a/asthma

[5] Cincinnati Children's Hospital Medical Center. Meet the Team | Asthma Center. https://www.cincinnatichildrens.org/service/a/asthma/team
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies Cleveland Clinic as the nonprofit academic medical center headquartered in Ohio. Agent B incorrectly identifies Cincinnati Children's Hospital, failing the core entity requirement. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A covers all required checklist elements—pediatric asthma experts helping children who gasp and wheeze, easing anxiety for parents, lifelong primary care, treatment of sinus infections and high blood pressure, and preventive screenings. Agent B discusses pediatric asthma services but does not establish the required lifelong primary care scope and even admits the absence of specific details about sinus infections, high blood pressure, and preventive screenings.

Finally, User Experience: Agent A provides a structured, comprehensive, and cohesive explanation with clear linkage between services and institutional identity. Agent B is incomplete, speculative in parts, and fails to directly answer the full query. Because Agent A is correct and complete while Agent B fails both the core entity and major checklist elements, Agent A is MUCH_BETTER.
